page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1.large is an x86_64 architecture virtual server instance belonging to the ecs.se1 server family. Powered by the KVM hypervisor, this instance features 2 dedicated physical cores running on an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor clocked at 2.5 GHz. It is equipped with 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. The processor includes a 35 MB L3 cache and supports instruction sets such as Advanced Vector Extensions (AVX and AVX512F), AES cryptography, and Intel Turbo Boost technology. This instance does not include local storage or bundled GPU hardware, and its baseline network bandwidth is rated at 0 Gbps.

In benchmark evaluations, the ecs.se1.large demonstrates modest performance characteristics. Single-core CPU performance via stress-ng is rated as weak, scoring 1098.69, while multi-core performance falls into the bottom 10% with a score of 1458.9. Memory bandwidth tests show 81.55 GB/sec for cached small blocks and 10.74 GB/sec for uncached large blocks, reflecting lower-tier memory throughput. Cryptographic and database operations also align with entry-level capabilities, with Passmark database operations scoring 552.23 and Redis SET operations reaching 1.43 million operations per second.

Given its high memory-to-vCPU ratio combined with lower-tier compute and memory bandwidth performance, the ecs.se1.large represents a qualitative tradeoff. It offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high CPU throughput or local disk I/O. This server is appropriate for lightweight database caching, small-scale Redis deployments, basic web serving, and memory-heavy but compute-light background processes.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1.large is an x86_64 memory-optimized virtual server powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at 2.5 GHz. It features 2 dedicated physical cores, 16.0 GB of RAM, and no local storage or GPU accelerators. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, the instance provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Benchmark results indicate modest performance, with stress-ng single-core and multi-core metrics placing in the lower percentiles. Memory bandwidth is limited, reaching 10.74 GB/sec for uncached large blocks, and Redis SET operations perform at 1.43 million operations per second. This instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-demanding workloads with low compute requirements, such as lightweight database caching, basic web serving, and memory-heavy background tasks.
